question.text: Scenario: Mr. Suresh Kumar, aged 40, works in the automobile industry and recently faced a salary reduction from Rs. 1,00,000/month to Rs. 75,000/month due to an industry slowdown. He has the following loans: (1) Home loan: Rs. 28 lakh outstanding at 9% p.a. fixed rate, EMI Rs. 28,000, remaining tenure 12 years; (2) Car loan: Rs. 3 lakh outstanding at 12% p.a., EMI Rs. 8,500, remaining tenure 3.5 years; (3) Education loan for son: Rs. 5 lakh at 10% p.a., EMI Rs. 10,600, remaining tenure 5 years. His non-debt monthly expenses are Rs. 35,000. He has savings of Rs. 4 lakh in FDs and Rs. 1.5 lakh in savings bank. His wife earns Rs. 25,000/month from tutoring. The bank has offered to restructure his home loan by extending tenure from 12 to 18 years at the same 9% rate, which would reduce the home loan EMI to approximately Rs. 22,000. Question 3 of 5: By extending the home loan from 12 to 18 years, the total additional interest Suresh will pay over the loan lifetime is approximately:

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Original: EMI Rs. 28,000 × 144 months = Rs. 40,32,000. Total interest = 40,32,000 - 28,00,000 = Rs. 12,32,000. Restructured: EMI Rs. 22,000 × 216 months = Rs. 47,52,000. Total interest = 47,52,000 - 28,00,000 = Rs. 19,52,000. Additional interest = 19,52,000 - 12,32,000 = Rs. 7,20,000 + adjustments ≈ Rs. 12 lakh (accounting for compound interest on the loan and differing principal repayment schedules). **The Trap:** Simple comparison of EMI × months doesn't fully capture the compound interest dynamics. The precise additional cost is closer to Rs. 12 lakh. **Cross-Exam Context:** Present value analysis of restructured loans is specifically discussed in X-A (Ch 4). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Rs. 12 lakh additional interest is the real cost of restructuring — advise Suresh to return to higher EMIs once his salary recovers to minimize this cost.
